repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Ref: #38525

## Problem
Our JSON-RPC client is oriented towards LSP implementation and contains logic for message framing. This belongs to the LSP extension beneath JSON-RPC, known as the [“base protocol"](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#baseProtocol). This logic should be decoupled to improve clarity.

## Solution
Extract `vim.MessageStream` for message framing. Since this function is used in the test suite, it may be beneficial as a standalone module; furthermore, perhaps it and `vim._transport` could also be used for `vim.net`? cc @yochem

comment_data.body:
This seems a bit like a performance regression in regards to string allocations.

Wouldn't it be possible to basically keep the coroutine as decoder abstraction? (previous `request_parser_loop`)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -76,6 +76,32 @@ local function get_content_length(header)
   error('Content-Length not found in header: ' .. header)
 end
 
+--- Parse one framed message from `strbuf`.
+---
+--- Returns a body after consuming one full frame,
+--- returns `nil` if more bytes are needed.
+--- May raises an error if the buffered data is not a valid frame.
+---
+---@param strbuf vim._core.stringbuffer
+---@return string?
+local function parse_content_length(strbuf)
+  local header_end = strbuf:tostring():find('\r\n\r\n', 1, true)
+  if not header_end then
+    return nil
+  end
+
+  local header = strbuf:get(header_end + 1)
+  strbuf:skip(2) -- skip past header boundary
+
+  local content_length = get_content_length(header)
+  if strbuffer.len(strbuf) < content_length then
+    strbuf:set(header .. '\r\n' .. strbuf:tostring())

comment_data.body: What's the format for and why is both parse/format parameterized here if these are always the same arguments for the concrete lsp.rpc cases?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -273,9 +269,11 @@ local Client = {}
 
 ---@package
 ---@param dispatchers vim.lsp.rpc.Dispatchers
----@param transport vim.lsp.rpc.Transport
+---@param transport vim.Transport
+---@param parse fun(buf: vim._core.stringbuffer): string?
+---@param format fun(msg: string): string

comment_data.body:
The main reason for no longer using coroutines is that if we allow users to customize the parse function (to support formats other than ContentLength), it would be difficult for them to write their own parse without knowing exactly how we invoke it because of the coroutine. Therefore, I made this function "interface" no longer depend on coroutines.

Considering that Lua doesn't have true multithreading, coroutines probably shouldn't offer much of a performance boost here, right? I'm actually not entirely sure. I think the original intent behind relying on coroutines was to preserve state across multiple calls in the read loop, but I have implemented it here so that state is maintained through classes and objects instead.

comment_data.body: This part is pending further refactoring in the next step; #38525 will expose `parse` and `format` as parameters. `parse` is used to strip the header from the message stream and return the body, while `format` does exactly the opposite by adding a header to the incoming body.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -273,9 +269,11 @@ local Client = {}
 
 ---@package
 ---@param dispatchers vim.lsp.rpc.Dispatchers
----@param transport vim.lsp.rpc.Transport
+---@param transport vim.Transport

+---@param format fun(msg: string): string

comment_data.body:
The performance remark was about the `header .. '\r\n' .. strbuf:tostring()` allocations.

The coroutine remark wasn't about multithreading, but about the coroutine's interface because it fits a basic decoder's requirements: Get some input and produce 0 to n outputs based on that input.

> , it would be difficult for them to write their own parse without knowing exactly how we invoke it because of the coroutine

Not sure I follow why it would be difficult. The coroutine will get some string as input, and must yield the decoded messages as output (or yield empty if it needs more data). How exactly the decoding works is implementation specific and depends on the (wire) protocol.

All that would be needed is to parameterize the `request_parser_loop`.

comment_data.body:
But why does this need to be parameterized for the concrete _lsp_ Client instance? The decoding and encoding of messages shouldn't be customizable for it. 

I guess the `format` confused me a bit because in network abstractions I'm more used to `decode`/`encode` terminology
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -273,9 +269,11 @@ local Client = {}
 
 ---@package
 ---@param dispatchers vim.lsp.rpc.Dispatchers
----@param transport vim.lsp.rpc.Transport
+---@param transport vim.Transport
+---@param parse fun(buf: vim._core.stringbuffer): string?
+---@param format fun(msg: string): string

comment_data.body:
> But why does this need to be parameterized for the concrete _lsp_ Client instance? The decoding and encoding of messages shouldn't be customizable for it.

To be more precise, the instance here is a JSON-RPC Client specifically provided for LSP. The reason for parameterizing it here is that once we turn this `rpc.lua` into a generic JSON-RPC Client, there will no longer be a dedicated LSP JSON-RPC Client. Instead, there will only be a generic JSON-RPC client, and LSP will use this generic client by passing in `parse` and `format` functions that handle `ContentLength`.

> I guess the `format` confused me a bit because in network abstractions I'm more used to `decode`/`encode` terminology

`decode`/`encode` are indeed more appropriate in most cases, but since our messages are JSON objects, I'm concerned that using `decode`/`encode` instead of `parse`/`format` might cause some confusion, given that we have `json.decode` and `json.encode` to handle JSON objects. Do you think `encode`/`decode` is still better, or should I change them to a different name?

+---@param format fun(msg: string): string

comment_data.body: Perhaps I overestimated the difficulty of requiring users to implement coroutines; I have changed it back to the previous pattern using `coroutine`. I also added a comment, which may clearly explain its purpose.

comment_data.body: This is the wrong abstraction as you've split the coroutine use over 2 places. All the coroutine logic should be inside `parse()` and act as an iterator function.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -186,7 +182,66 @@ function TransportConnect:terminate()
   end
 end
 
+---@class vim.MessageStream
+---@field private co thread
+---@field private parse fun()
+---@field private on_read fun(err: string?, data: string?)
+---@field private on_error fun(err: any)
+---@field feed fun(self: vim.MessageStream, err: string?, data: string?)
+---@field format fun(msg: string): string
+local MessageStream = {}
+
+--- Create a message stream from a coroutine parser.
+---
+--- The parser receives transport data from `coroutine.yield()`
+--- and may yield a message body when a full message is available.
+--- A nil yield means it needs more transport data.
+--- Parser errors are reported through `on_error`.
+---
+---@param parse fun()
+---@param format fun(msg: string): string
+---@param on_read fun(err: string?, data: string?)
+---@param on_error fun(err: any)
+---@return vim.MessageStream
+function MessageStream.new(parse, format, on_read, on_error)

comment_data.body:
Yes, this is why I stopped using coroutines previously, also mentioned in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39000#discussion_r3126154737. But implementing it according to your suggestion is essentially inlining `parse` into `MessageStream:feed`, then treating `feed` as `parse`. This would mean that if message formats other than `ContentLength` exist, we would have to rewrite all the logic from scratch, and `MessageStream` no longer makes sense.   

However, I created this `MessageStream` abstraction for other purposes, not to replace the coroutine, just found it is clearer without coroutines, and then stopped using it. There are several purposes of this abstraction:

1. Match the signature of `MessageStream:feed(err, data)` with the transport's `on_read(err, data)`, replacing `create_read_loop`. The goal is to make `MessageStream` a pluggable layer between the actual application and the `transport`. For example:
    - Passing `on_read = function(err, data)` directly to the `transport` will cause it to be called every time data is read from the `transport`, then passing the data directly;
    - By adding `message_stream` to pass `on_read = function(err, data)`, this will ensure that the function is called with the complete data only when the data received in the `transport` forms a full frame. 

    This makes implementing a callback to consuming data clearer because `on_read` becomes our convention to do it. This convention originates from `uv.uv_stream_t:read_start`, then used by `transport`.
2. Clearer error handling. The `on_error` of `MessageStream` is only called when a parsing error occurs, reading errors should be handled in `on_read`, just like using `transport` without `message_stream`. This should be clearer than `create_read_loop`, which has no hint when `on_error` is called. More importantly, the previous error handling relied on some specific error kinds (`vim.lsp.rpc.ClientErrors`) from `rpc.lua`; we had to avoid that.
3. Better to handle invalid input. The existing implementation terminates `transport` if an error occurs when parsing. By having `message_stream` store the `parse` function, we can recreate a new one here if the `coroutine` dies due to a parse error. This should improve our tolerance for malformed data. Ref: #35743. This PR does not implement this but would benefit for implementing this.

Overall, the existing message framing seemed simple but actually had a lot of logic mixed into it. I believe yesterday's version without coroutines is the cleanest abstraction, though it does have some performance regression.

comment_data.body: It seems like chasing this refactor with a new abstraction isn't worth it. Maybe consider a different route. Duplicating code is ok sometimes, and further consolidations can happen later.

+---@return vim.MessageStream

comment_data.body:
For #38525, the critical issue was that logic from the `rpc.lua` layer was mixed into message framing errors. If you search for `on_error` in `rpc.lua` on the `master` branch, you will find that this name appears in far too many places. Sometimes it is `self:on_error`, sometimes `dispatchers.on_error`, and other times an anonymous function wrapping `on_error` with custom logic; they all have subtle differences in their signatures, and one may call the other one. This makes it difficult to figure out when they are actually called and at which layer the error occurs. 

I originally intended to wait until a demand for framing methods other than `ContentLength` arose before adding such an abstraction. However, the messiness of the error handling led me to believe that abstracting it in advance would make the review easier and keep each commit atomic. I prefer to eliminate over-abstraction through refactoring after #38525, which is more conducive to subsequent progress. However, I have no objections to the implementation that could be improved here.

@@ -226,35 +236,25 @@ end
 function M.create_read_loop(handle_body, on_exit, on_error)
   on_exit = on_exit or function() end
   on_error = on_error or function() end
-  local co = coroutine.create(message_decoder)
-  coroutine.resume(co)
-  return function(err, chunk)
-    if err then
-      on_error(err, M.client_errors.READ_ERROR)
-      return
-    end
-
-    if not chunk then
-      on_exit()
-      return
-    end
-
-    if coroutine.status(co) == 'dead' then
-      return
-    end
-
-    while true do
-      local ok, res = coroutine.resume(co, chunk)
-      if not ok then
-        on_error(res, M.client_errors.INVALID_SERVER_MESSAGE)
-        break
-      elseif res then
-        handle_body(res)
-        chunk = ''
+  local message_stream = vim_transport.MessageStream.new(
+    parse_content_length,
+    format_message_with_content_length,
+    function(err, chunk)
+      if err then
+        on_error(err, M.client_errors.READ_ERROR)

comment_data.body:
The current naming convention seems to be aimed at preventing redundant nesting, such as using `vim.Pos` instead of `vim.pos.Pos`. 

The reason I didn't move it under `vim.net` as previously discussed is that other parts of `vim.net` do not currently use these functions, which may be a little confusing.

comment_data.body: some parts of this pr call `M.client_errors`, others call `client_errors`, why?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -226,35 +236,25 @@ end
 function M.create_read_loop(handle_body, on_exit, on_error)
   on_exit = on_exit or function() end
   on_error = on_error or function() end
-  local co = coroutine.create(message_decoder)
-  coroutine.resume(co)
-  return function(err, chunk)
-    if err then
-      on_error(err, M.client_errors.READ_ERROR)
-      return
-    end
-
-    if not chunk then
-      on_exit()
-      return
-    end
-
-    if coroutine.status(co) == 'dead' then
-      return
-    end
-
-    while true do
-      local ok, res = coroutine.resume(co, chunk)
-      if not ok then
-        on_error(res, M.client_errors.INVALID_SERVER_MESSAGE)
-        break
-      elseif res then
-        handle_body(res)
-        chunk = ''
+  local message_stream = vim_transport.MessageStream.new(
+    parse_content_length,
+    format_message_with_content_length,
+    function(err, chunk)
+      if err then
+        on_error(err, M.client_errors.READ_ERROR)

comment_data.body:
`vim.net` is a place for things to live, it's ok if things in there don't use `vim.net.Transport`.

`vim.Pos` is a fundamental primitive, whereas `vim.Transport` doesn't seem like such a thing, it's much more of a special case.

OTOH, it's true that we don't really have strong conventions (nor a contract) for class names, and we can always change them later.

 local log = require('vim.lsp.log')
+local strbuffer = require('vim._core.stringbuffer')

---- @field write fun(self: vim.lsp.rpc.Transport, msg: string)
---- @field is_closing fun(self: vim.lsp.rpc.Transport): boolean
---- @field terminate fun(self: vim.lsp.rpc.Transport)

comment_data.body: Originally, they all used `M.client_errors`; so this is my typo. `M.client_errors` added fields to enable reverse lookup functionality; I think I should unify them to use `M.client_errors` in this PR.

comment_regular_data.body: I hesitated for a long time on the interface design of this message framer, and this might not be its final state. However, in this PR, I have decoupled the message framing logic from RPC and ensured full compatibility, and this new module is private. I suppose we can leave it like this for now and make adjustments in #38525, or redesign it once we have other framing requirements.

> perhaps it and vim._transport could also be used for vim.net? cc @yochem

I haven't looked too much into what _transport.lua does, but if it's networking and can be reused in other places then yes, it should be in `vim.net`.

Considering that we do not currently require a complex networking library, I have refactored the "message framing" layer into a minimal abstraction. This abstraction also simplifies our previous coroutine-based parsing approach and may help improve our tolerance for malformed input data in the future.
hoping that @mfussenegger @lewis6991 can review this, else ping me in a few days
For reviewers, I added the non-coroutine version (with a slight improvement to avoid one allocation)in a single commit so you can compare the two variants, with or without the coroutine.
Along with #39389, I have replaced the pattern that doesn't use `coroutine` here with one that avoids new string allocations. Do you think this would be better?

To briefly summarize the goals of this PR (as my previous explanation was a bit lengthy):

1. **Decouple framing from JSON-RPC:** Currently, our JSON-RPC implementation strictly expects LSP-style framing (`Content-Length` headers). Standard JSON-RPC makes no such assumptions; many other protocols use `\n` as delimiters.
2. **Clearer error handling:** Search the error handler `on_error` in `rpc.lua`, you can find that there are a bunch of references here, but no idea where the error `on_error` would occur. This practice of throwing an error in one place and handling it in another is equivalent to using a `goto`.
3. **Improve reusability:** "Extracting a message body from a stream" is a distinct, reusable concept. Our test suite already relies on this (via `create_read_loop`), but the hardcoded `Content-Length` parsing and coupled error handling make it inflexible.

A significant implementation change here is dropping the `coroutine`. The benefits include:

1. Errors inside a `coroutine` will make it dead, then lose the state it keeps. This PR keeps the state by an object, which should make it easier to understand and robust to errors.
2. It is difficult to parameterize a `parse` function to define an alternative parsing method other than `Content-Length` when using a coroutine, as `parse` must be aware of the existence of the `coroutine`, hindering support for alternative parsing methods.
3. The overall logic without `coroutine` is less complex.

Conversely, retaining `coroutine` offers no real advantage. In summary, I believe this new abstraction is straightforward and highly effective for the problems it solves.

@justinmk, I would appreciate a review when you have a moment.
Will review this ASAP, but want to ask just based on the PR title, does it make sense for `vim.iter` to be the interface for streaming? Ideally we shouldn't need another "pipe" abstraction.

It's unlikely. This abstraction exposes a method (`feed(err, data)`) that allows data to be passed in by calling it, and outputs the framed data by calling another callback (`on_read(err, data)`), which makes this pipe different from `vim.iter`. 

Another difference is error handling. `vim.iter` itself does not provide an interface for error handling, whereas `MessageStream` provides an `on_error`, allowing the caller to define how errors are handled.

> > I guess the `format` confused me a bit because in network abstractions I'm more used to `decode`/`encode` terminology
> 
> `decode`/`encode` are indeed more appropriate in most cases, but since our messages are JSON objects, I'm concerned that using `decode`/`encode` instead of `parse`/`format` might cause some confusion, given that we have `json.decode` and `json.encode` to handle JSON objects. Do you think `encode`/`decode` is still better, or should I change them to a different name?

decode/encode always depend on the context. i don't think changing the names helps much, and it hurts discovery. 


> A significant implementation change here is dropping the `coroutine`. The benefits include:
> 
> 1. Errors inside a `coroutine` will make it dead, then lose the state it keeps. This PR keeps the state by an object, which should make it easier to understand and robust to errors.
> 2. It is difficult to parameterize a `parse` function to define an alternative parsing method other than `Content-Length` when using a coroutine, as `parse` must be aware of the existence of the `coroutine`, hindering support for alternative parsing methods.
> 3. The overall logic without `coroutine` is less complex.
> 
> Conversely, retaining `coroutine` offers no real advantage

SGTM
